title: The Transformer Weight Footprint
scenario: You are asked in a phone screen: a 7-billion-parameter Transformer is loaded onto an H100 GPU in FP16 precision. How much VRAM do the weights alone consume, and does the model fit?

details.common_mistake: Forgetting that FP16 means 2 bytes per parameter, not 1, or confusing weight memory with total training memory (which includes optimizer states and activations).
details.realistic_solution: 14 GB. Each of the 7B parameters occupies 2 bytes in FP16, so 7B * 2 = 14 GB. The H100 has 80 GB HBM3, so the weights alone fit comfortably. However, training requires ~4x more for Adam optimizer states.
details.napkin_math: 7B params * 2 bytes/param (FP16) = 14 GB. H100 has 80 GB HBM3, so 14/80 = 17.5% of VRAM for weights alone.

title: The Tokenizer Throughput Ceiling
scenario: You are profiling an LLM training pipeline on an H100. The GPU utilization is only 40%. A colleague suggests the tokenizer running on CPU might be the bottleneck. Roughly how many tokens per second must the CPU tokenizer produce to keep an H100 busy on a 7B model?

details.common_mistake: Assuming the tokenizer is always fast enough because 'it is just string processing.' In reality, the H100 can consume tokens at enormous rates, and a single-threaded Python tokenizer can easily fall behind.
details.realistic_solution: Approximately 70,000 tokens/sec. A 7B model requires ~14 GFLOPS per token (2 * 7B FLOPs). The H100 delivers 989 TFLOPS FP16, so it can theoretically process 989e12 / 14e9 ~ 70,000 tokens/sec. The CPU tokenizer must match this rate.
details.napkin_math: FLOPs per token = 2 * 7B = 14 GFLOPS. H100 peak = 989 TFLOPS. Max throughput = 989e12 / 14e9 ~ 70,600 tokens/sec.

title: The PCIe Transfer Bottleneck
scenario: You are training an image classification model with batch size 2048. Each image is 224x224x3 in FP32. How long does it take to transfer a single batch from CPU to GPU over PCIe Gen5 x16 (64 GB/s unidirectional)?

details.common_mistake: Not computing the actual batch size in bytes. Candidates often guess 'milliseconds' without doing the math, or forget that FP32 means 4 bytes per value.
details.realistic_solution: About 19 ms. Each image is 224 * 224 * 3 * 4 = 602,112 bytes (~0.6 MB). A batch of 2048 images = 2048 * 0.6 MB = 1.23 GB. Over 64 GB/s PCIe: 1.23 / 64 = 19.3 ms. This is fast enough for most training loops but must be overlapped with compute.
details.napkin_math: Image = 224 * 224 * 3 * 4 bytes = 0.6 MB. Batch = 2048 * 0.6 MB = 1.23 GB. Transfer time = 1.23 GB / 64 GB/s = 19.3 ms.

title: The Kernel Fusion Memory Savings
scenario: You are interviewing for an ML infrastructure role. The interviewer asks: a LayerNorm operation without kernel fusion requires four separate HBM read/write passes over a tensor. With fusion, it requires only two. For a hidden dimension of 4096, sequence length of 2048, in FP16, how much HBM traffic does fusion eliminate?

details.common_mistake: Thinking operator fusion is only about reducing kernel launch overhead. The dominant benefit is eliminating redundant memory traffic to HBM, which is the actual bottleneck for memory-bound operations.
details.realistic_solution: Fusion saves ~50% of HBM traffic. The tensor is 4096 * 2048 * 2 bytes = 16.8 MB. Unfused: 4 passes * 16.8 MB = 67 MB of traffic. Fused: 2 passes * 16.8 MB = 34 MB. Savings = 33 MB, a 50% reduction.
details.napkin_math: Tensor = 4096 * 2048 * 2 bytes = 16.8 MB. Unfused = 4 * 16.8 = 67 MB. Fused = 2 * 16.8 = 34 MB. Savings = 50%.

title: The Gradient AllReduce Time
scenario: You are running data-parallel training of a 7B model across 8 H100 GPUs in a single node connected via NVLink 4.0 (900 GB/s bidirectional). Gradients are in FP32. How long does the AllReduce synchronization take?

details.common_mistake: Using FP16 gradient size instead of FP32, or forgetting the 2*(N-1)/N ring AllReduce overhead factor. Some candidates also confuse bidirectional bandwidth with unidirectional.
details.realistic_solution: About 54 ms. Gradient size = 7B * 4 bytes = 28 GB. Ring AllReduce sends 2*(N-1)/N * data volume = 2*(7/8)*28 = 49 GB over the ring. At 900 GB/s NVLink: 49/900 ~ 54 ms.
details.napkin_math: Gradients = 7B * 4 bytes (FP32) = 28 GB. Ring AllReduce factor = 2*(8-1)/8 = 1.75. Total traffic = 1.75 * 28 = 49 GB. Time = 49 / 900 = 54.4 ms.

title: The ECC Bit Error Reality
scenario: You are asked in an interview: an H100 has 80 GB of HBM3 memory. Assuming a raw bit error rate of 1e-15 per bit per hour (before ECC correction), roughly how many uncorrected bit errors would you expect per hour without ECC?

details.common_mistake: Assuming memory errors are so rare they can be ignored. At datacenter scale with hundreds of GPUs, even 'rare' errors become frequent events that corrupt training runs.
details.realistic_solution: About 0.00064 errors per hour per GPU, or roughly one error every 1,562 hours. 80 GB = 640 billion bits. At 1e-15 errors/bit/hour: 640e9 * 1e-15 = 6.4e-4 errors/hour. This seems small per GPU, but across 1,000 GPUs in a cluster, you get ~0.64 errors/hour. ECC is essential.
details.napkin_math: 80 GB = 80e9 * 8 = 640e9 bits. Errors/hour = 640e9 * 1e-15 = 6.4e-4. Hours between errors = 1/6.4e-4 = 1,562 hours per GPU. At 1,000 GPUs: one error every ~1.6 hours.

title: The Checkpoint Size Math
scenario: You are training a 13B parameter model using Adam optimizer. Your manager asks how much disk space a single training checkpoint requires. What is the total checkpoint size including weights (FP16), Adam optimizer states (FP32 momentum + variance), and gradients (FP32)?

details.common_mistake: Only counting the weight memory and forgetting that Adam stores two additional FP32 copies (momentum and variance) per parameter, plus gradients. The optimizer states dominate checkpoint size.
details.realistic_solution: 182 GB. Weights: 13B * 2 bytes = 26 GB. Adam momentum: 13B * 4 bytes = 52 GB. Adam variance: 13B * 4 bytes = 52 GB. Gradients: 13B * 4 bytes = 52 GB. Total: 26 + 52 + 52 + 52 = 182 GB.
details.napkin_math: Weights (FP16) = 13B * 2 = 26 GB. Adam states (FP32) = 13B * 4 * 2 = 104 GB. Gradients (FP32) = 13B * 4 = 52 GB. Total = 26 + 104 + 52 = 182 GB.

title: The MoE Sparse Activation Ratio
scenario: You are evaluating a Mixture-of-Experts model with 47B total parameters: a 7B shared backbone and 8 expert FFN blocks of 5B parameters each, using top-2 routing. The interviewer asks you to explain the effective compute cost per token compared to a dense 47B model.

details.common_mistake: Thinking MoE models are 'just bigger' and require proportionally more compute. The key insight is that only a subset of experts activate per token, so compute scales with active parameters, not total parameters.
details.realistic_solution: Only 36% of parameters are active per token. The backbone (7B) always runs, plus top-2 of 8 experts (2 * 5B = 10B), giving 17B active parameters. FLOPs per token ~ 2 * 17B = 34 GFLOPS, versus 2 * 47B = 94 GFLOPS for a dense 47B model. You get the representational capacity of 47B at the compute cost of 17B.
details.napkin_math: Active params = 7B backbone + 2 * 5B experts = 17B. Ratio = 17B / 47B = 36.2%. FLOPs per token: MoE = 2 * 17B = 34 GFLOPS vs Dense = 2 * 47B = 94 GFLOPS. Compute savings = 64%.
options: A) 100% — all experts must process every token
B) ~36% — only backbone + top-2 experts activate
D) ~50% — half the experts activate on average

title: The Cross-Node AllReduce Cost
scenario: You are training a 13B model across 4 nodes (8 GPUs each, 32 total) connected by InfiniBand NDR (400 Gb/s = 50 GB/s per link). Gradients are FP32. Estimate the ring AllReduce time, assuming cross-node InfiniBand is the bottleneck.

details.common_mistake: Using NVLink bandwidth for the cross-node calculation. NVLink only connects GPUs within a node. Cross-node communication must traverse InfiniBand, which is ~18x slower than NVLink 4.0.
details.realistic_solution: About 2 seconds. Gradient size = 13B * 4 bytes = 52 GB. Ring AllReduce factor = 2*(32-1)/32 = 1.94. Total traffic per GPU = 1.94 * 52 = 100.8 GB. At 50 GB/s IB bandwidth: 100.8 / 50 = 2.0 seconds. This is why gradient compression and overlap with compute are critical at scale.
details.napkin_math: Gradients = 13B * 4 bytes = 52 GB. Ring factor = 2*(31/32) = 1.94. Traffic = 1.94 * 52 = 100.8 GB. Time = 100.8 / 50 GB/s = 2.0 seconds.

title: The NVLink vs PCIe Tensor Parallel Gap
scenario: You are deciding whether tensor parallelism is viable across PCIe-connected GPUs versus NVLink-connected GPUs. Tensor parallelism requires an all-reduce of the activation tensor after every transformer layer. For batch=32, seq=2048, hidden=4096 in FP16, compare the per-layer communication time over NVLink 4.0 (450 GB/s unidirectional) vs PCIe Gen5 x16 (32 GB/s unidirectional).

details.common_mistake: Assuming PCIe is 'only a little slower.' The ~14x bandwidth gap makes tensor parallelism over PCIe impractical for latency-sensitive inference, adding milliseconds of overhead per layer.
details.realistic_solution: Activation tensor = 32 * 2048 * 4096 * 2 bytes = 537 MB. NVLink: 537 MB / 450 GB/s = 1.2 ms. PCIe: 537 MB / 32 GB/s = 16.8 ms. Over a 32-layer model, PCIe adds 32 * 16.8 = 537 ms vs NVLink's 38 ms. This is why tensor parallelism requires NVLink.
details.napkin_math: Activation = 32 * 2048 * 4096 * 2 = 537 MB. NVLink time = 537 / 450,000 = 1.19 ms. PCIe time = 537 / 32,000 = 16.8 ms. Ratio: 14.1x slower on PCIe.
options: A) NVLink is ~2x faster — minor difference
B) NVLink is ~5x faster — noticeable but manageable
C) NVLink is ~14x faster — PCIe makes tensor parallelism impractical
D) NVLink is ~100x faster — different technology class

title: The Flash Attention Memory Cliff
scenario: You are serving a model with 32 attention heads and sequence length 8192 in FP16. Your colleague asks why standard attention runs out of memory but Flash Attention does not. Calculate the attention matrix memory for standard attention versus Flash Attention's working memory.

details.common_mistake: Thinking Flash Attention is just 'faster attention.' The key insight is that it changes the memory complexity from O(n^2) to O(n) by tiling the computation and never materializing the full attention matrix in HBM.
details.realistic_solution: Standard attention materializes a (seq x seq) matrix per head: 32 heads * 8192 * 8192 * 2 bytes = 4.29 GB just for the attention scores. Flash Attention tiles the computation in SRAM and never writes the full matrix to HBM, reducing working memory to ~0.2 GB — a 21x reduction.
details.napkin_math: Standard: 32 * 8192^2 * 2 bytes = 32 * 134M * 2 = 4.29 GB. Flash Attention working memory ~ 32 * 8192 * 128 * 2 * 3 (Q,K,V tiles) = 0.20 GB. Reduction = 4.29 / 0.20 = ~21x.
options: A) Standard uses 0.5 GB vs Flash uses 0.1 GB — modest savings
B) Standard uses 4.3 GB vs Flash uses ~0.2 GB — ~21x reduction
C) Standard uses 16 GB vs Flash uses 4 GB — 4x reduction
D) Both use the same memory; Flash is only faster, not smaller

title: The Cluster MTBF Math
scenario: You are planning a 2-week training run on a 1,000-GPU cluster. Each GPU has a mean time between failures (MTBF) of 10,000 hours. How often should you expect a GPU failure to interrupt training?

details.common_mistake: Thinking 10,000-hour MTBF means the cluster is safe for weeks. Per-device reliability compounds multiplicatively: with 1,000 devices, the cluster MTBF drops to just ~10 hours.
details.realistic_solution: Expect a failure roughly every 10.5 hours. P(any failure in 1 hour) = 1 - (1 - 1/10000)^1000 = 1 - 0.9048 = 9.5%. Expected time to first failure = 1/0.095 = 10.5 hours. Over a 2-week run (336 hours), you will face ~32 interruptions. Checkpointing every 10 minutes is essential.
details.napkin_math: P(all survive 1hr) = (1 - 1/10000)^1000 = 0.905. P(failure) = 9.5%. MTBF_cluster = 1/0.095 = 10.5 hours. Failures in 2 weeks = 336/10.5 = 32 failures.
options: A) Once per month — 10,000-hour MTBF is very reliable
B) Once per week — failures are uncommon
C) Once every ~10 hours — cluster reliability compounds
D) Once per hour — GPUs are inherently unreliable

title: The Async Checkpoint Overhead
scenario: You are training a 70B model and need to checkpoint frequently given your cluster's ~10-hour MTBF. A full checkpoint (FP16 weights + FP32 Adam states) is 700 GB. Your NVMe drives write at 3 GB/s. A synchronous checkpoint would stall training for how long, and how does async checkpointing fix this?

details.common_mistake: Assuming checkpointing is free because 'we just save to disk in the background.' Synchronous checkpointing pauses all GPU compute. Even async requires a fast memory copy to CPU RAM, which still takes ~14 seconds.
details.realistic_solution: Synchronous write: 700 GB / 3 GB/s = 233 seconds (~4 minutes) of stalled training per checkpoint. With a 30-second training step, this is catastrophic. Async checkpointing first copies to CPU RAM at ~50 GB/s (14 seconds of GPU pause), then writes to NVMe in the background while training continues. This reduces overhead from 233s to 14s per checkpoint.
details.napkin_math: Checkpoint = 70B * (2 + 4 + 4) bytes = 700 GB. Sync NVMe write = 700/3 = 233s. Async CPU copy = 700/50 = 14s. Training step = ~30s. Sync overhead = 233/30 = 777%. Async overhead = 14/30 = 47%.
options: A) ~2 seconds — NVMe is fast enough
B) ~23 seconds — manageable with less frequent checkpoints
C) ~233 seconds — catastrophic without async checkpointing
D) ~2,333 seconds — checkpointing is practically impossible

title: The Datacenter PUE Cooling Budget
scenario: You are designing the power budget for a new AI training cluster with 10,000 H100 GPUs (700W TDP each). The facility targets a PUE of 1.10. Your CFO asks for the annual electricity cost and wants to understand where the non-GPU power goes. Wholesale electricity is $0.05/kWh. Analyze the total power budget and cost structure.

details.common_mistake: Only counting GPU power and ignoring PUE overhead. Even a best-in-class PUE of 1.10 means 10% overhead for cooling, networking, and storage — which at this scale is 700 kW of 'invisible' power draw.
details.realistic_solution: GPU power = 10,000 * 700W = 7 MW. Total facility power at PUE 1.10 = 7.7 MW. The 700 kW overhead covers cooling (CRAC units, chilled water), networking switches, storage, and lighting. Annual energy = 7,700 kW * 8,760 hours = 67.5 GWh. Annual cost = 67.5M kWh * $0.05 = $3.4M. The cooling overhead alone costs $306K/year. At higher PUE (1.4 for average datacenter), overhead would be 2.8 MW and $1.2M/year.
details.napkin_math: GPU power = 10,000 * 700W = 7,000 kW = 7 MW. Facility = 7 MW * 1.10 = 7.7 MW. Cooling overhead = 700 kW. Annual = 7,700 * 8,760 = 67.5 GWh. Cost = 67.5M * $0.05 = $3.4M/year.

title: The TCO Per Token Analysis
scenario: Your VP of Engineering asks you to evaluate whether to build or buy LLM inference capacity. An 8xH100 server costs $250K with a 3-year amortization. Power is 5.6 kW at $0.10/kWh. Each GPU produces ~5,000 tokens/sec for your 13B model. Assuming 70% utilization, compute the all-in cost per million tokens and compare it to a cloud API charging $0.50 per million tokens. When does build vs. buy make sense?

details.common_mistake: Only comparing hardware CAPEX to API pricing without accounting for energy, cooling, staffing, and utilization. Also, candidates overestimate utilization — 70% is optimistic for most organizations.
details.realistic_solution: Server CAPEX amortized: $250K / 3 years = $83.3K/year. Energy: 5.6 kW * 8,760 hrs * $0.10 = $4,906/year. 3-year TCO ~ $265K. Token output: 8 GPUs * 5,000 tok/s * 0.70 util * 86,400 s/day * 365 * 3 = 2.65 trillion tokens. Cost = $265K / 2.65M million-tokens = $0.10 per million tokens. This is 5x cheaper than $0.50/M API pricing. But add staffing ($150K/year), networking, and facility costs — real cost is ~$0.20-0.30/M tokens. Build makes sense above ~50M tokens/day sustained demand.
details.napkin_math: CAPEX = $250K / 3yr = $83.3K/yr. Energy = 5.6 kW * 8760 * $0.10 = $4.9K/yr. 3yr TCO = $265K. Tokens = 40,000 tok/s * 0.70 * 94.6M s = 2.65T tokens. Raw cost = $0.10/M tokens. With ops overhead: ~$0.25/M tokens vs $0.50/M API.

title: The Spot Instance Training Economics
scenario: Your team needs 1,000 GPU-hours to fine-tune a model. On-demand H100 instances cost $3.00/GPU/hour. Spot instances cost $0.90/GPU/hour but have a 10% preemption rate per hour. Preemptions require rolling back to the last checkpoint (saving every 10 minutes) and restarting, adding roughly 15% overhead in total GPU-hours consumed. Evaluate whether spot instances are worth the operational complexity.

details.common_mistake: Either blindly choosing spot for the 70% discount without accounting for preemption overhead, or avoiding spot entirely because 'it is unreliable.' The correct answer requires quantitative analysis of the cost-reliability tradeoff.
details.realistic_solution: On-demand: 1,000 hrs * $3.00 = $3,000. Spot with overhead: 1,000 hrs * 1.15 (overhead) * $0.90 = $1,035. Net savings = $1,965 (65.5%). The 15% overhead comes from checkpoint-restart cycles: with 10% preemption rate and 10-min checkpoint intervals, you lose ~5 min of work per preemption plus restart overhead. Spot is clearly worth it for fault-tolerant training workloads. The key requirement is robust checkpointing infrastructure — without it, preemptions cause full restarts and savings evaporate.
details.napkin_math: On-demand = 1000 * $3.00 = $3,000. Spot = 1000 * 1.15 * $0.90 = $1,035. Savings = $1,965 = 65.5%. Break-even overhead = (3.00/0.90 - 1) = 233% — spot saves money even with 3.3x overhead.

title: The Carbon-Aware Scheduling Tradeoff
scenario: Your organization has committed to reducing the carbon footprint of ML training. A 1,000 GPU-hour training job at 700W per GPU consumes 700 kWh. Your two datacenter regions have grid carbon intensities of 400 gCO2/kWh (coal-heavy) and 50 gCO2/kWh (renewable-heavy), but the renewable region has 20% less GPU capacity and 15% higher latency to your data lake. Evaluate whether carbon-aware scheduling is worth the performance tradeoff.

details.common_mistake: Treating carbon reduction as a binary choice — either you care about carbon or you optimize for performance. In practice, carbon-aware scheduling can achieve 80-90% carbon reduction with modest performance impact by scheduling batch training (not latency-sensitive inference) to low-carbon regions.
details.realistic_solution: Coal region: 700 kWh * 400 g/kWh = 280 kg CO2. Renewable region: 700 kWh * 50 g/kWh = 35 kg CO2. Carbon reduction = 87.5% (245 kg saved). The 20% capacity reduction means the job takes ~20% longer (1,200 GPU-hours effective), and data loading is 15% slower. For batch training, this adds a few hours to a multi-day job — negligible. For latency-sensitive inference, the tradeoff is harder to justify. The optimal strategy: route training and batch jobs to low-carbon regions, keep inference in the region closest to users.
details.napkin_math: Coal: 700 kWh * 400 g = 280 kg CO2. Renewable: 700 kWh * 50 g = 35 kg CO2. Savings = 245 kg = 87.5% reduction. Time penalty: ~20% longer training. Carbon per dollar: at $0.05/kWh, job costs $35 either way, but saves 245 kg CO2.
